summary: Spolsky reviews Scott Rosenberg's 'Dreaming in Code,' which chronicles the Chandler project's failure to ship meaningful software despite years of effort and millions of dollars. He uses a brilliant analogy about how human eyes create an illusion of complete vision through rapid page-faulting to explain why software teams overestimate how well they understand their own grand visions. He argues Chandler failed because its design was described in adjectives ('revolutionary') rather than concrete specifics, and because the team hired programmers before doing real design work. Spolsky also critiques the project's key ideas—peer-to-peer sync, 'no silos,' and natural language dates—as either too hard, already done, or fundamentally flawed from a usability perspective.
key_points: Your mind tricks you into thinking you have a complete picture of a software project when you actually only have vague impressions, leading teams to skip critical design work
Hiring programmers before completing design is a compounding mistake—designing software as a team in meetings is far harder than one person working alone with paper
Specs described in adjectives ('revolutionary,' 'extremely cool') rather than concrete specifics are a red flag that real design hasn't happened
The 'No Silos' concept of merging email and calendar appeals to architecture astronauts but destroys usability by eliminating real-world metaphors users rely on
Open source volunteers don't materialize for apps that don't do anything yet—they contribute to working software where they can scratch their own itches
money_quotes: One of the unfortunate side effects is that your mind gets into a bad habit of overestimating how clearly it understands things. It always thinks it has The Big Picture even when it doesn't.
The only thing harder than trying to design software is trying to design software as a team.
Did you ever try to write poetry in a committee meeting? It's like a bunch of fat construction guys trying to write an opera while sitting on the couch watching Baywatch.
I don't know about you, but I can't code 'revolutionary.' I need more details to write code.

Whenever the spec describes the product in terms of adjectives ('it will be extremely cool') rather than specifics ('it will have brushed-aluminum title bars and all the icons will be reflected a little bit, as if placed on a grand piano') you know you're in trouble.

The way you make users understand your program model is with metaphors. When you try to combine two very dramatically different real-world items into the same kind of thing in the user interface, usability suffers because there's no longer a real-world metaphor that applies.
When you have an app that doesn't do anything yet, nobody finds it itchy. They're not using it. So you don't get volunteers.
key_insight: Software projects fail when teams mistake a vague, adjective-driven vision for actual design, diving into code before anyone has worked out the concrete details of what they're building.
